steps.title: Fastest path: mark the figure first
steps.content: For coordinate-circle questions, read the center and radius from the equation or graph the circle as a check. For diagram facts, mark equal radii, diameters, tangents, and arcs before doing algebra.

steps.title: Step 1
steps.content: Read circle $M$: $(x-3)^{2}+(y-6)^{2}=4$ is in standard form $(x-h)^{2}+(y-k)^{2}=r^{2}$, so center $= (3,6)$ and $r_{M} = \sqrt{4} = 2$.

steps.title: Step 2
steps.content: Circle $P$ has the same center $(3,6)$ and radius $r_{P} = 2 \times r_{M} = 2 \times 2 = 4$, so $r_{P}^{2} = 16$.

steps.title: Step 3
steps.content: Circle $P$'s equation is $(x-3)^{2}+(y-6)^{2}=16$. The formula $(x-3)^{2}+(y-6)^{2}=8$ uses $r^{2}=8$, giving $r=2\sqrt{2}\approx2.83$, not twice circle $M$'s radius of 2. The formula $(x-3)^{2}+(y-6)^{2}=16$ has center $(3,6)$ and $r^{2}=16$, so $r=4=2\times2$. The formulas with center $(6,12)$ are eliminated because circle $P$ must keep center $(3,6)$. That leaves B.
